### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description In Data Science and AI education, teaching students how to clean and preprocess "dirty data" (handling missing values, detecting outliers, removing duplicates, and fixing text typos) is a critical skill. Currently, professors and students struggle to find realistic, messy datasets for practice. When using clean benchmark datasets (like Iris or Titanic), users have to write long, repetitive custom scripts from scratch just to artificially mess up the data for testing and educational purposes. There is no built-in, one-stop tool in Pandas to inject controlled chaos into a DataFrame. ### Feature Description import pan

question: What feature request is described in ENH: Improve missing column KeyError message in DataFrame access?
answer: ENH: Improve missing column KeyError message in DataFrame access. ### Feature Type - [ ] Adding new functionality to pandas - [x] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description # Problem Description Currently, when users attempt to access a column that does not exist in a DataFrame using: df["Age"] Pandas raises a generic error message: KeyError: 'Age' While technically correct, this message provides very limited context for user
ground_truth_chunks: ENH: Improve missing column KeyError message in DataFrame access

### Feature Type - [ ] Adding new functionality to pandas - [x] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description # Problem Description Currently, when users attempt to access a column that does not exist in a DataFrame using: df["Age"] Pandas raises a generic error message: KeyError: 'Age' While technically correct, this message provides very limited context for users, especially beginners. The current behavior does not: explain why the error occurred, show available columns, help users identify possible typos, improve debugging experience. This can make debugging slower and more frustrating for new users working with DataFrames. Example Reproduction import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({ "Name": ["Pradip", "Rahul"] }) print

The current behavior does not: explain why the error occurred, show available columns, help users identify possible typos, improve debugging experience. This can make debugging slower and more frustrating for new users working with DataFrames. Example Reproduction import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({ "Name": ["Pradip", "Rahul"] }) print(df["Age"]) Current output: KeyError: 'Age' # Expected Behavior The error message should provide more contextual information. The same handling is missing from group_cumpr

context:
BUG: Conversion from np.ma.masked_array to pd.array does not preserve missing values
### Pandas version checks - [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas. - [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description NumPy [masked arrays](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/maskedarray.generic.html#what-is-a-masked-array) are used to define arrays with missing values, similar to pandas extension arrays. However, converting to `pd.array` currently ignores the mask completely and masked values appear as regular values. Converting to `pd.Series` does respect the missing values, but the dtype is converted to `float` and the original dtype is not preserved in all cases. This behavior is inconsistent with user expectations and with how `pd.Series` handles masked

question: What issue is discussed in DEP: update of fsspec 2026.4 broke compressed csv reading?
answer: DEP: update of fsspec 2026.4 broke compressed csv reading. From https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65433, the update of fsspec from 2026.2.0 to 2026.4 caused some tests to fail (that PR added a temporary upper pin).
ground_truth_chunks: DEP: update of fsspec 2026.4 broke compressed csv reading

From https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/pull/65433, the update of fsspec from 2026.2.0 to 2026.4 caused some tests to fail (that PR added a temporary upper pin). From looking at the PRs included in that release, this is likely caused by https://github.com/fsspec/filesystem_spec/pull/2012 Example failure (https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/actions/runs/25182285458/job/73830335550): ``` __________________ test_read_csv_chained_url_no_error[infer] ___________________ [gw2] linux -- Python 3.14.4 /home/runner/work/pandas/pandas/.pixi/envs/py314/bin/python datapath = <function datapath.<locals>.deco at 0x7f208c290050> compression = 'infer' @td.skip_if_no("fsspec") @pytest.mark.parametrize("compression", [None, "infer"]) def test_read_csv_chained_url_no_error(datapath, compression): # GH 601

question: What performance issue is described in PERF: skip to_datetime cache=True overhead for input shapes where it cannot help?
answer: PERF: skip to_datetime cache=True overhead for input shapes where it cannot help. #### Summary `to_datetime(..., cache=True)` (the default) unconditionally runs the `_maybe_cache` machinery — `Series(dtype=object)` construction + `should_cache` which iterates up to 500 elements of the input — even on inputs where `_convert_listlike_datetimes` is going to dispatch to a non-parsing path or a path where caching has been measured to never help.
ground_truth_chunks: PERF: skip to_datetime cache=True overhead for input shapes where it cannot help

#### Summary `to_datetime(..., cache=True)` (the default) unconditionally runs the `_maybe_cache` machinery — `Series(dtype=object)` construction + `should_cache` which iterates up to 500 elements of the input — even on inputs where `_convert_listlike_datetimes` is going to dispatch to a non-parsing path or a path where caching has been measured to never help. On these inputs `cache=True` is strictly slower than `cache=False`, by margins ranging from a few percent to **~520×** at the worst. All five "no-help" conditions are identifiable in O(1) time, before any sampling, just by looking at `arg.dtype`, the `unit` kwarg, and the `format` kwarg. #### Affected input shapes (all O(1) to detect) 1. `unit is not None` — input is dispatched to `_to_datetime_with_unit`, which is a vectorized cast;
context:
PERF: skip to_datetime cache=True overhead for input shapes where it cannot help
#### Summary `to_datetime(..., cache=True)` (the default) unconditionally runs the `_maybe_cache` machinery — `Series(dtype=object)` construction + `should_cache` which iterates up to 500 elements of the input — even on inputs where `_convert_listlike_datetimes` is going to dispatch to a non-parsing path or a path where caching has been measured to never help. On these inputs `cache=True` is strictly slower than `cache=False`, by margins ranging from a few percent to **~520×** at the worst. All five "no-help" conditions are identifiable in O(1) time, before any sampling, just by looking at `arg.dtype`, the `unit` kwarg, and the `format` kwarg. #### Affected input shapes (all O(1) to detect) 1. `unit is not None` — input is dispatched to `_to_datetime_with_unit`, which is a vectorized cast; caching cannot help. 2. `arg.dtype` is `np.dtype("M8[*]")` — already datetime64; `_convert_listlike_datetimes` short-circuits. 3. `arg.dtype` is `DatetimeTZDtype` — already tz-aware; short-circuits.

BUG: DataFrame/Series.rename silently downcasts nullable index/column dtypes to NumPy dtypes
### Pandas version checks - [x] I have checked that this issue has not already been reported. - [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [latest version](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/index.html) of pandas. - [x] I have confirmed this bug exists on the [main branch](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/dev/getting_started/install.html#installing-the-development-version-of-pandas) of pandas. ### Reproducible Example ### Issue Description `DataFrame.rename()` and `Series.rename()` do not preserve nullable extension dtypes on index/column labels. In the reproduced cases with nullable integer and floating dtypes, renaming labels silently downcasts the result to the corresponding NumPy dtype. The root cause appears to be `Index._transform_index()`, which reconstructs the transformed index from a plain Python list instead of preserving/casting through the original `ExtensionArray` dtype. ### Expected Behavior `rename()` should preserve the original index dtype after relabeling. ### In

question: What issue is discussed in API: Index.take discards fill_value and raises unnecessarily?
answer: API: Index.take discards fill_value and raises unnecessarily. `Index.take` has two bugs when `allow_fill=True` and `fill_value` is passed explicitly: ### 1.
ground_truth_chunks: API: Index.take discards fill_value and raises unnecessarily

`Index.take` has two bugs when `allow_fill=True` and `fill_value` is passed explicitly: ### 1. `fill_value` is silently discarded The caller's `fill_value` is ignored; `self._na_value` is always substituted: ### 2. `_can_hold_na` gate raises on integer dtypes The caller provided a valid fill value that the dtype can hold, but `Index.take` rejects it because `_can_hold_na` is False for integer dtypes. ### Consequences These force internal callers to bypass `Index.take` and work on the underlying arrays directly: - **`frame.py` idxmin/idxmax**: `algorithms.take(index._values, ...)` instead of `index.take(...)` - **`reshape/merge.py`**: appends a sentinel value to the index, with a comment: *"We do not use allow_fill and fill_value because it throws a ValueError on integer indices"* - **`inde
context:
API: Index.take discards fill_value and raises unnecessarily
`Index.take` has two bugs when `allow_fill=True` and `fill_value` is passed explicitly: ### 1. `fill_value` is silently discarded The caller's `fill_value` is ignored; `self._na_value` is always substituted: ### 2. `_can_hold_na` gate raises on integer dtypes The caller provided a valid fill value that the dtype can hold, but `Index.take` rejects it because `_can_hold_na` is False for integer dtypes. ### Consequences These force internal callers to bypass `Index.take` and work on the underlying arrays directly: - **`frame.py` idxmin/idxmax**: `algorithms.take(index._values, ...)` instead of `index.take(...)` - **`reshape/merge.py`**: appends a sentinel value to the index, with a comment: *"We do not use allow_fill and fill_value because it throws a ValueError on integer indices"* - **`indexes/base.py` reset_index**: take-then-putmask as two operations - **`io/formats/excel.py`**: guards `allow_fill` on `levels._can_hold_na` - **`indexes/multi.py`**: `algos.take_nd(lev._values, ...)` in

question: What feature request is described in ENH: Simplify reading vertically stacked CSV tables?
answer: ENH: Simplify reading vertically stacked CSV tables. ### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [x] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description The overall goal is reading vertically stacked CSV files, with multiple tables.
ground_truth_chunks: ENH: Simplify reading vertically stacked CSV tables

### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [x] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description The overall goal is reading vertically stacked CSV files, with multiple tables. There is no difficulty in writing such a file with pandas (just append) or reading and splitting horizontally stacked CSV files (but they can be inefficient and more complicated to write). This is not technically a CSV format (according to RFC 4180, since column sizes differ) but a very slight generalisation of it. It doesn't really have a name, but has been re-invented several times, with different ways of determining where to split the table. I think the only form that really needs supporting are known specified lines, e.g. [[''], ['C
context:
ENH: Simplify reading vertically stacked CSV tables
### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [x] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description The overall goal is reading vertically stacked CSV files, with multiple tables. There is no difficulty in writing such a file with pandas (just append) or reading and splitting horizontally stacked CSV files (but they can be inefficient and more complicated to write). This is not technically a CSV format (according to RFC 4180, since column sizes differ) but a very slight generalisation of it. It doesn't really have a name, but has been re-invented several times, with different ways of determining where to split the table. I think the only form that really needs supporting are known specified lines, e.g. [[''], ['Col1', 'Col2']] would split on blank rows but also on Col1,Col2 rows. I had considered "when the number of columns changes", but I think that's a strictly less useful format (you can't have multiple ta

question: What feature request is described in ENH: Add DataFrame.memory_report() for actionable dtype optimization suggestions?
answer: ENH: Add DataFrame.memory_report() for actionable dtype optimization suggestions. ### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description DataFrame.memory_usage() tells you how much memory a DataFrame uses, but gives no guidance on why or what to do about it.
ground_truth_chunks: ENH: Add DataFrame.memory_report() for actionable dtype optimization suggestions

### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description DataFrame.memory_usage() tells you how much memory a DataFrame uses, but gives no guidance on why or what to do about it. Users who want to optimize memory must: - Manually inspect each column's dtype - Know the integer/float downcast rules by heart - Manually check cardinality for object columns - Write their own loops to apply changes This is a very common pain point — especially for data engineers and analysts working with medium-to-large datasets who hit memory pressure and have no built-in tool to diagnose it. ### Feature Description A new method DataFrame.memory_report() that: 1. Analyzes every column's curre
context:
ENH: Add DataFrame.memory_report() for actionable dtype optimization suggestions
### Feature Type - [x] Adding new functionality to pandas - [ ] Changing existing functionality in pandas - [ ] Removing existing functionality in pandas ### Problem Description DataFrame.memory_usage() tells you how much memory a DataFrame uses, but gives no guidance on why or what to do about it. Users who want to optimize memory must: - Manually inspect each column's dtype - Know the integer/float downcast rules by heart - Manually check cardinality for object columns - Write their own loops to apply changes This is a very common pain point — especially for data engineers and analysts working with medium-to-large datasets who hit memory pressure and have no built-in tool to diagnose it. ### Feature Description A new method DataFrame.memory_report() that: 1. Analyzes every column's current dtype and actual value range 2. Suggests the smallest safe dtype for each column 3. Explains why each suggestion is safe (value range, cardinality ratio) 4. Returns a rich MemoryReport object that
